title: Version Control in context
objective: Use tools or labs so the topic becomes practical, not abstract.
checkpoint: Can connect automation ideas to real analyst workflows.
resource_names: Google Course 7: Automate Cybersecurity Tasks with Python
plain_language_intro: Today covers Version Control within google course 7: python automation (part 2).
mini_lecture_markdown:
The week focus is Google Course 7: Python automation (part 2), and today's objective is use tools or labs so the topic becomes practical, not abstract. The main terms in scope are Version Control. The focus is on what each one does, how it operates, and how it affects the rest of the system.

Version Control is system for tracking code changes and collaboration history within scripting and systems automation workflows. It records changes as commits, tracks history, and supports branching or collaboration on code and content. Mechanically, it stores changes as commits, tracks diffs between versions, and merges or reverts work through branch history. Together, these terms describe where work happens, how state changes, and what the visible outcome looks like when the system is operating normally.

Build or finish the portfolio automation task and test it carefully with sample inputs. The goal is to connect the concept to actual behavior, output, or system state.

key_distinctions: Version Control vs Documentation — Version Control: System for tracking code changes and collaboration history within scripting and systems automation workflows. Documentation: Written record of configurations, procedures, findings, or changes that explains what was done and why.
Purpose vs mechanism — be able to separate what Version Control is for from how it actually performs that job.
Recognition vs diagnosis — knowing the term name is not enough; know what evidence would make you pick it in a scenario.
mental_model_markdown: Use a pipeline model for this day: input arrives, a component or service processes it, state changes occur, and an output or symptom becomes visible to the user, administrator, or analyst. Version Control: stores changes as commits, tracks diffs between versions, and merges or reverts work through branch history That sequence is the main mental model for the topic.
